statement: The ancestor of modern corn, teosinte, had ears with only 5–12 kernels per cob — compared to the 800+ kernels on a modern corn cob.
wowFactor: Ancient corn had barely 10 kernels — 10,000 years of farming turned it into the giant cob we eat today!
explanation: Teosinte (Zea mays ssp. parviglumis) is the wild grass from Mexico from which corn (Zea mays) was domesticated. Wild teosinte ears hold only 5–12 hard kernels enclosed in stony casings, making them nearly useless as food. After 10,000 years of selective breeding, modern corn cobs typically have 800 or more kernels arranged in neat rows. This transformation represents one of the most dramatic artificial selection events in agricultural history.
gaiaComment: From 12 tiny hard kernels to 800 juicy ones — corn's makeover took 10,000 years and rewrote what a plant could become.
quizQuestion: How many kernels per ear did teosinte — the wild ancestor of corn — typically have?

statement: The transformation of teosinte into modern corn required changes in only about 5 key genes — one of the smallest genetic changes to produce such a dramatic agricultural transformation.
wowFactor: Just 5 gene changes turned a scraggly wild grass into the world's most productive crop — nature's tiniest revolution!
explanation: Genetic analysis has identified approximately 5 major loci (gene regions) that account for most of the key morphological differences between teosinte and modern corn: kernel row number, cob architecture, seed casing hardness, branching pattern, and tillering. Research by geneticist John Doebley and colleagues showed that relatively few mutations in regulatory genes — genes that control other genes — produced the dramatic phenotypic shift. This makes maize domestication a landmark case study in evolutionary genetics.
gaiaComment: Five gene changes from wild grass to world crop — corn proves that small mutations can have enormous consequences.
quizQuestion: Approximately how many key gene changes transformed teosinte into modern corn?

statement: Corn was domesticated from teosinte in Mexico's Balsas River Valley approximately 9,000 years ago, and spread to all of the Americas before European contact.
wowFactor: 9,000 years ago in one Mexican river valley, one plant began a journey that would feed two entire continents!
explanation: The Balsas River Valley of the modern Mexican state of Guerrero is identified through genetic and archaeological evidence as the geographic origin of corn domestication. From this origin, corn cultivation spread northward into modern-day United States (including the American Southwest and Mississippi Valley) and southward through Central and South America over thousands of years. By the time Europeans arrived in the Americas in the 15th–16th centuries, corn was grown from Canada to Argentina.
gaiaComment: One Mexican valley, one wild grass, 9,000 years — and corn conquered two continents without a single European helping.
quizQuestion: In which region of Mexico was corn first domesticated from teosinte?
correctAnswer: The Balsas River Valley (in modern Guerrero state)
distractors: The Valley of Mexico (Mexico City region)

statement: The Three Sisters — corn, beans, and squash — were cultivated together by Indigenous peoples of North America, forming a sustainable agricultural system where each plant benefits the others.
wowFactor: Three crops planted together help each other grow — Native Americans invented companion planting 2,000 years ago!
explanation: In the Three Sisters system: corn grows tall providing a climbing pole for beans; beans fix atmospheric nitrogen, fertilizing the soil for corn and squash; squash sprawls on the ground, its large leaves shading the soil to retain moisture and suppress weeds, while its prickly stems deter pests. This polyculture system, practiced across North America for over 2,000 years, produces more food per acre than growing each crop separately and requires no external fertilizer.
gaiaComment: Corn, beans, and squash — three plants that figured out how to take care of each other while feeding humans too.
quizQuestion: What are the Three Sisters plants used in traditional Indigenous North American agriculture?

statement: Corn kernels get their color from anthocyanin pigments — different gene combinations produce red, blue, purple, yellow, white, and even multi-colored 'Indian corn' varieties.
wowFactor: Corn kernels are naturally rainbow-colored — the full color spectrum comes from the same pigments found in blueberries!
explanation: Anthocyanins are water-soluble pigments found in many fruits, vegetables, and flowers. In corn, multiple genes control anthocyanin production in the aleurone (outer seed layer) and pericarp (hull). Different allele combinations produce colors from white (no pigment) through yellow (carotenoids), red, blue, and purple. 'Glass gem corn' — a popular modern multi-colored variety — dramatically displays this genetic diversity, with each kernel on a cob potentially carrying different allele combinations.
gaiaComment: The genetics of corn color is basically art — each kernel on a rainbow corn cob is its own genetic experiment.
quizQuestion: What pigment compounds give corn kernels their range of colors including red, blue, and purple?

statement: Corn (maize) is the world's largest crop by total production volume — over 1.2 billion tonnes are grown annually, surpassing rice and wheat.
wowFactor: Corn is the number one crop on Earth by sheer weight — over a billion tonnes every year!
explanation: According to FAO data, global corn production exceeds 1.2 billion tonnes annually — more by weight than rice (~500 million tonnes) or wheat (~760 million tonnes). However, unlike rice and wheat which are primarily eaten directly by humans, about 40% of corn production goes to animal feed, 15% to ethanol fuel, and only about 10% is eaten directly as food by humans. The US produces about 30–35% of the world's corn.
gaiaComment: The world's biggest crop, and most of it feeds cars and livestock — corn's productivity outran human appetite entirely.
quizQuestion: Approximately how many tonnes of corn are produced globally each year, making it the world's largest crop by volume?

statement: The Maya creation myth in the Popol Vuh states that humans were made from corn dough — reflecting how central maize was to Mesoamerican civilization.
wowFactor: The Maya believed humans were literally made of corn — and that's not a metaphor to them, it was sacred truth!
explanation: The Popol Vuh, the K'iche' Maya creation epic, describes multiple attempts by the gods to create humans. After failures with mud and wood, the successful creation was made from yellow and white corn dough. The Maya word for corn (ixim) and for person were closely linked, and 'people of corn' was a self-description. This belief reflected corn's absolute centrality to Maya subsistence — life, religion, calendar, and cosmology were all intertwined with the cultivation of maize.
gaiaComment: The Maya said humans were made of corn — and given how dependent civilization was on maize, they weren't entirely wrong.
quizQuestion: According to the Maya Popol Vuh creation story, what were humans made from?

statement: The oldest evidence of popcorn dates back 7,000 years, found at archaeological sites in Peru — making popcorn one of humanity's oldest snack foods.
wowFactor: Popcorn is 7,000 years old! Ancient Peruvians were snacking on popcorn millennia before movie theaters existed.
explanation: Charred popcorn kernels were found at archaeological sites in Peru dating back approximately 7,000 years, making them among the earliest evidence of corn use in South America. Popcorn varieties of corn have especially hard, moisture-containing endosperm that, when heated rapidly, builds steam pressure until the kernel explodes. Evidence of popcorn has also been found at sites in New Mexico dating back 5,600 years. Popcorn appears to have been one of the earliest processed corn foods.
gaiaComment: Seven-thousand-year-old popcorn — the ancient Peruvians invented movie night 7,000 years before movies.
quizQuestion: How old is the oldest archaeological evidence of popcorn, found at sites in Peru?

statement: Corn is a grass (family Poaceae) that has been so heavily domesticated that it cannot survive in the wild — it depends entirely on humans to reproduce.
wowFactor: Corn is so domesticated it cannot even drop its own seeds — it literally cannot survive without human help!
explanation: Unlike its ancestor teosinte, whose seed casings shatter at maturity to scatter seeds, modern corn husks hold all kernels tightly on the cob. Without a human to peel back the husk and scatter kernels, corn cannot seed the next generation. This is a result of 10,000 years of selecting for seeds that stay on the plant for easy harvesting — an adaptation perfectly suited to farming but fatal for survival without farmers. Corn is one of the clearest examples of a plant that co-evolved with humanity.
gaiaComment: Corn and humanity struck a deal 9,000 years ago: we feed you, you feed us — and corn held up its end of the bargain completely.
quizQuestion: Why can modern corn not survive without human intervention?
correctAnswer: Because its husks hold all kernels tightly on the cob, preventing seeds from dispersing naturally
distractors: Because corn only grows in artificially irrigated fields
Because corn lacks the roots needed to anchor in wild soil
Because corn seeds die unless soaked in water first
Because corn requires hand pollination from a different plant
Because corn cannot survive frost without human shelter
Because corn needs processed fertilizer to germinate
Because corn seeds are too large to be eaten and spread by birds
Because corn requires human-made soil amendments to set seed

statement: Nixtamalization — soaking corn in an alkaline solution like limewater — unlocks niacin (vitamin B3) from the grain and dramatically improves its nutritional value; without this process, corn-dependent populations develop pellagra.
wowFactor: Ancient Mesoamericans discovered a food processing trick that unlocks hidden vitamins in corn — Europe ignored it and paid with disease!
explanation: In its raw form, niacin in corn is chemically bound and cannot be absorbed by the human body. Mesoamerican peoples discovered that soaking corn in limewater (calcium hydroxide solution) before grinding — nixtamalization — breaks these bonds, releasing niacin and improving protein quality. When Europeans brought corn to Europe and Africa without this processing technique, populations that became corn-dependent developed pellagra (niacin deficiency disease), causing skin lesions, dementia, and death. The Maya never suffered pellagra because they always used nixtamalization.
gaiaComment: The Maya knew how to unlock corn's full nutrition — and when Europeans copied the crop without the recipe, thousands died of pellagra.
quizQuestion: What ancient Mesoamerican food processing technique unlocks niacin (vitamin B3) from corn, preventing pellagra?
correctAnswer: Nixtamalization — soaking corn in an alkaline limewater solution
